bins.name: Distributed capacity for constraint relief and rapid deployment
bins.desc: The filers emphasize demand flexibility, batteries, and distributed generation as available today and deployable in months rather than years. These resources provide utility-scale capacity equivalent to large traditional plants and should be leveraged to mitigate constraints caused by new loads and accelerate interconnection timelines.
quotes: the Distributed Capacity Parties respectfully urge the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("FERC" or "Commission") in any subsequent rulemaking to direct the Regional Transmission Organizations/Independent System Operators ("RTO/ISOs") to develop mechanisms for granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable and/or dispatchable distributed assets (aka Distributed Capacity) capable of mitigating potential system constraints attributable to the new large loads.

The ANOPR Seventh Principle should include incremental Distributed Capacity in the form of load flexibility, storage, and/or generation capacity whose location can address capacity and transmission constraints projected to be caused by the new large loads, even if such capacity is located on the distribution system.

Distributed capacity assets are installed today across critical footprints, are readily installable en masse, and may be aggregated into utility-scale assets on a timeline of months rather than years.
In 2023 alone, the Southwest Power Pool ("SPP") added approximately 431 MW new demand response resource capability, equivalent to a large power plant.
Another 500 MW power plant could be added by interconnecting to the distribution system a mere 200 batteries of 1-5 MW size.
hyperscalers could more than meet their total planned capacity needs by paying for battery storage as well as rooftop solar for homes well suited for it.

If the Commission truly wants to bring all American resources to bear in service of interconnecting large loads, Distributed Capacity is one of the only resource types that can be enlisted on a timeline that matches the urgency of the challenge at a scale that counts.

new capacity supporting large loads need not be physically co-located at the facility, but can be distributed across the geographic area within which the new load will impact system constraints, provided that the system operator has the requisite level of control to integrate the facility into operations and system planning.

ANOPR Principle Seven should be broadened to leverage all available resources. The Principle as written does not recognize the reduced impact of an interconnecting load that brings on (contracts for) demand-side flexibility or capacity not physically "within the fence-line" of the facility but still appropriately located to relieve constraints.

capacity—including batteries, generation, and demand response (DR)—should be geographically transferable in the form of capacity credits as defined by the wholesale market, or within the TOs system planning paradigm.

bins.name: Electrical proximity and joint study of capacity with loads
bins.desc: Large loads should be studied jointly with nearby or electrically proximate distributed capacity, even if not physically at the same site, to exploit network savings. Such resources can be distributed across the load's impact zone and recognized via capacity credits within wholesale market frameworks.
quotes: The ANOPR Seventh Principle should include incremental Distributed Capacity in the form of load flexibility, storage, and/or generation capacity whose location can address capacity and transmission constraints projected to be caused by the new large loads, even if such capacity is located on the distribution system.
Another 500 MW power plant could be added by interconnecting to the distribution system a mere 200 batteries of 1-5 MW size.
New large loads that bring their own capacity—in whatever form—should be studied together with that capacity.
siting a new large load while considering the location of installed but underutilized grid resources and proposed aggregations of the same can save on network upgrades.

new capacity supporting large loads need not be physically co-located at the facility, but can be distributed across the geographic area within which the new load will impact system constraints, provided that the system operator has the requisite level of control to integrate the facility into operations and system planning.

ANOPR Principle Seven should be broadened to leverage all available resources. The Principle as written does not recognize the reduced impact of an interconnecting load that brings on (contracts for) demand-side flexibility or capacity not physically "within the fence-line" of the facility but still appropriately located to relieve constraints.

capacity—including batteries, generation, and demand response (DR)—should be geographically transferable in the form of capacity credits as defined by the wholesale market, or within the TOs system planning paradigm.

bins.name: Holistic study of loads and distributed capacity together
bins.desc: Large loads and their associated or nearby distributed capacity should be studied together as an integrated system to properly credit the capacity's role in mitigating transmission and capacity impacts.
quotes: New large loads that bring their own capacity—in whatever form—should be studied together with that capacity.
siting a new large load while considering the location of installed but underutilized grid resources and proposed aggregations of the same can save on network upgrades.

bins.name: Network upgrade cost reduction via distributed resources
bins.desc: Using installed or quickly deployable distributed resources to meet capacity and constraint needs can avoid or reduce costly network upgrades that would otherwise be required to serve large loads.
quotes: Distributed capacity assets are installed today across critical footprints, are readily installable en masse, and may be aggregated into utility-scale assets on a timeline of months rather than years.

If the Commission truly wants to bring all American resources to bear in service of interconnecting large loads, Distributed Capacity is one of the only resource types that can be enlisted on a timeline that matches the urgency of the challenge at a scale that counts.
siting a new large load while considering the location of installed but underutilized grid resources and proposed aggregations of the same can save on network upgrades.

bins.name: ANOPR Principle Seven expansion for expedited studies
bins.desc: The filers seek to broaden ANOPR Principle Seven to explicitly recognize that large loads bringing non-co-located but appropriately sited distributed capacity, including demand flexibility, deserve expedited interconnection treatment.
quotes: the Distributed Capacity Parties respectfully urge the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("FERC" or "Commission") in any subsequent rulemaking to direct the Regional Transmission Organizations/Independent System Operators ("RTO/ISOs") to develop mechanisms for granting expedited interconnection studies to large loads that provide curtailable and/or dispatchable distributed assets (aka Distributed Capacity) capable of mitigating potential system constraints attributable to the new large loads.

The ANOPR Seventh Principle should include incremental Distributed Capacity in the form of load flexibility, storage, and/or generation capacity whose location can address capacity and transmission constraints projected to be caused by the new large loads, even if such capacity is located on the distribution system.

ANOPR Principle Seven should be broadened to leverage all available resources. The Principle as written does not recognize the reduced impact of an interconnecting load that brings on (contracts for) demand-side flexibility or capacity not physically "within the fence-line" of the facility but still appropriately located to relieve constraints.

bins.name: Southwest Power Pool as example of demand response scale
bins.desc: The filers cite SPP's 2023 addition of 431 MW of demand response as evidence that distributed capacity can scale rapidly to utility-grade levels.
quotes: In 2023 alone, the Southwest Power Pool ("SPP") added approximately 431 MW new demand response resource capability, equivalent to a large power plant.
